text:
CT NECK AND CHEST WITH CONTRAST — SURVEILLANCE IMAGING

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Margarett Okonkwo is a 52-year-old female with a history of HPV-positive (p16+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, initially staged as T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition, Stage I for HPV-positive oropharynx) with involvement of a left level IIA cervical lymph node demonstrating central necrosis at diagnosis. She was treated with definitive concurrent intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT, 70 Gy in 35 fractions to the high-risk volume using simultaneous integrated boost technique) and Platinol chemotherapy (100 mg/m2, 2 full cycles plus 1 dose-reduced cycle at 75 mg/m2 due to nephrotoxicity and grade 3 mucositis). Treatment was completed approximately 6 months prior to this examination. Post-treatment PET-CT performed at approximately 10 weeks following chemoradiation completion demonstrated complete metabolic response at both the primary base of tongue site and the involved left cervical lymph node. She subsequently had her PEG tube removed and has returned to full oral intake. This CT of the neck and chest with contrast is performed as part of routine 6-month post-treatment surveillance.

COMPARISON:
Prior PET-CT performed approximately 10 weeks post-chemoradiation. CT neck and chest at the time of initial staging workup.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the neck and chest was performed following the administration of 100 mL of intravenous iodinated contrast (Omnipaque 350) with routine neck and portal venous phase chest acquisition. Images were reconstructed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes at 3 mm slice thickness. Soft tissue, bone, and lung windows were reviewed.

---

FINDINGS:

NASO- AND OROPHARYNX / PRIMARY SITE:
The left base of tongue is well visualized. There is no discrete enhancing mass or soft tissue thickening at the primary treatment site to suggest gross local residual or recurrent tumor. The base of tongue mucosa demonstrates mild, diffuse post-radiation changes with slightly increased T2 signal intensity of the adjacent soft tissue and mildly altered enhancement pattern of the lingual tonsil relative to the right side, consistent with expected post-treatment fibrosis and mucosal remodeling. The valleculae are patent bilaterally. No ulceration, nodularity, or submucosal mass identified. The pre-epiglottic and paraglottic fat planes are preserved. The epiglottis is normal in contour and thickness. The hypopharynx is unremarkable.

SALIVARY GLANDS:
The parotid glands are mildly reduced in volume bilaterally, somewhat more pronounced on the left, consistent with post-radiation atrophic change. No discrete parotid or submandibular gland masses. The left submandibular gland demonstrates mild reduction in size and slightly heterogeneous parenchymal texture relative to the right, compatible with prior radiation field effect. No ductal dilation or obstructing calculi. The right submandibular gland appears intact.

CERVICAL LYMPH NODES:
Left neck: The previously bulky, centrally necrotic left level IIA lymph node has markedly regressed. A small, oval, homogeneous lymph node remnant is identified at this station measuring approximately 8 mm in short-axis diameter (previously 2.7 cm at diagnosis with central necrosis; not measurable on the post-treatment PET-CT due to absent FDG uptake). The residual node demonstrates no internal necrosis, no rim enhancement, and no abnormal architecture to suggest viable nodal metastatic disease. Residual soft tissue stranding is noted in the left level II nodal region, consistent with post-treatment fibrotic change.

No enlarged or morphologically suspicious lymph nodes are identified in the right level I-VI stations, in the bilateral retropharyngeal space, in the left or right level III, IV, or V stations, or in the bilateral supraclavicular regions. No lymph nodes exceeding 1 cm in short-axis diameter are seen anywhere in the neck. The right neck remains clear.

LARYNX:
The laryngeal framework is intact. No cartilage erosion, edema, or suspicious mucosal lesion. True and false vocal cords appear symmetric and unremarkable. The piriform sinuses are patent bilaterally.

THYROID GLAND:
The thyroid gland is within the radiation field. It appears mildly reduced in volume bilaterally compared to the pre-treatment study, which is consistent with radiation-induced thyroiditis and volume loss. No discrete thyroid nodule, mass, or suspicious focal lesion identified. No tracheal deviation.

VASCULAR STRUCTURES:
The bilateral carotid arteries and jugular veins are patent with normal opacification on contrast-enhanced sequences. No evidence of radiation-induced arteriopathy at this time. No venous thrombosis.

BONE AND SOFT TISSUE:
The mandible and cervical spine are intact without lytic or blastic osseous lesion. No pathologic fracture. Mild degenerative changes of the cervical spine consistent with age. Post-radiation soft tissue changes are present in the left neck, with mild skin thickening, subcutaneous stranding, and soft tissue induration in the left submandibular, level II, and level III regions. These findings are in keeping with expected late radiation-related soft tissue fibrosis and are stable/slightly improved compared to early post-treatment imaging. No fluid collection or abscess.

---

CHEST:

LUNGS AND AIRWAYS:
The lungs are clear. No pulmonary nodules, masses, consolidation, or pleural effusion identified. No focal air space opacity. The central airways are patent. No hilar enlargement. The lung parenchyma appears normal bilaterally without interstitial thickening, ground-glass opacity, or bronchiectasis. No pleural disease.

MEDIASTINUM:
The mediastinum is normal in contour. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes identified. No anterior, middle, or posterior mediastinal mass. The thymus is not enlarged. The aorta and great vessels appear normal in caliber. The pericardium is unremarkable.

UPPER ABDOMEN (imaged on scout and lower chest cuts):
The upper abdominal organs are not fully evaluated on this dedicated neck-and-chest study. The visualized liver, spleen, and stomach are unremarkable in appearance without focal abnormality. No ascites.

BONY THORAX:
The ribs, sternum, clavicles, and visualized thoracic vertebrae are without suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesion. No pathologic fracture.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. NO EVIDENCE OF LOCAL RECURRENCE AT THE PRIMARY SITE: The left base of tongue demonstrates expected post-radiation mucosal and soft tissue changes without discrete enhancing mass, nodularity, or abnormal soft tissue thickening to suggest residual or recurrent tumor at the primary site. Findings are stable and consistent with complete treatment response.

2. MARKED REGRESSION OF PREVIOUSLY INVOLVED LEFT LEVEL IIA LYMPH NODE: The left level IIA node, which measured 2.7 cm with central necrosis at initial diagnosis, has reduced to a small 8 mm homogeneous, non-necrotic fibrotic remnant. No morphological features of viable nodal metastatic disease are identified.

3. NO NEW CERVICAL, RETROPHARYNGEAL, OR MEDIASTINAL LYMPHADENOPATHY: The neck is otherwise clear bilaterally without new or suspicious nodal disease.

4. NO PULMONARY METASTASES OR THORACIC DISEASE: The chest is clear without evidence of hematogenous metastatic spread.

5. POST-RADIATION SOFT TISSUE AND SALIVARY GLAND CHANGES: Mild soft tissue fibrosis of the left neck, left parotid and submandibular gland volume loss, and mild thyroid gland volume reduction are present, all consistent with expected sequelae of prior chemoradiation to the oropharynx and bilateral neck.

Overall, these findings are in keeping with durable radiographic complete response at 6 months following completion of concurrent chemoradiation for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. No evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease.

Dictated and interpreted by: Attending Radiologist, Head and Neck Imaging Section
Electronically signed.
